3297.text: A Document Production Order will direct that a party must do one or more of the following things:

3298.text: (1) produce documents or classes of documents specified in the order;

3299.text: (2) carry out a search to the extent stated in the order;

3300.text: (3) produce any documents located as a result of that search;

3301.text: (4) identify documents or classes of documents which were, but are no longer, in the party’s possession, custody and control and explain, to the best of the party’s knowledge and belief, what has happened to them.

3303.text: The party from whom a Document Production Order is sought should provide to the applicant and to the Court information as to the factors listed in Rule 28.21 and his document retention policy, to the extent such information is relevant to the application and has not already been provided. At the hearing of an application for a Document Production Order, the Court may take into account the factors listed in Rule 28.21 as well as the width of the request and the conduct of the parties.

3305.text: If the propriety of an objection can only be determined by review of the document , the Judge hearing the objection may refer the objection to another Judge to review any such document and determine the objection. To the extent that the objection is upheld by the other Judge , the Court may determine that the other Judge will not disclose to the other parties the contents of the document reviewed and will take no further part in the case, either for the purpose of the hearing of applications or as the Judge at trial, unless the parties agree otherwise.

3307.text: Compliance with a Document Production Order must be verified by a Document Production Statement in the form set out in Schedule B to this Part.

3309.text: A Document Production Statement is a statement made by a party:

3310.text: (1) setting out the extent of the search that has been made to locate documents which he is required to produce;

3311.text: (2) certifying that he understands the duty to search for and produce documents ; and

3312.text: (3) certifying that to the best of his knowledge he has carried out that duty.

3314.text: Where the party making the Document Production Statement is a company, firm, association or other organisation, the statement must also:

3315.text: (1) identify the person making the statement; and

3316.text: (2) explain why he is considered an appropriate person to make the statement.

3318.text: (1) The parties’ obligation to produce documents in response to a Document Production Request or pursuant to a Document Production Order is a continuing one. Where a party subsequently comes into possession of further documents falling within the scope of such a request or order, the party must notify the requesting party of that fact and either produce the document or object to its production in accordance with RDC 28.26.

3319.text: (2) This rule shall not apply where the party has previously objected to production on grounds which apply to the new document and such objection has not been challenged by the other parties or has been upheld by the Court.

3322.text: The parties may agree in writing, or the Court may direct, that production of documents shall take place in stages.

3323.text: Production Of Documents Before Proceedings Start

3325.text: An application for production of documents before proceedings have started under these Rules must be made in accordance with Part 8 and supported by evidence.

3327.text: The Court may only make an order where:

3328.text: (1) the respondent is likely to be a party to subsequent proceedings;

3329.text: (2) the applicant is also likely to be a party to those proceedings;

3330.text: (3) if proceedings had started, the Court would make a Document Production Order directing the production of the documents or classes of documents of which the applicant seeks production; and

3331.text: (4) production before proceedings have started is desirable in order to:

3332.text: (a) dispose fairly of the anticipated proceedings; (b) assist the dispute to be resolved without proceedings; or (c) save costs.

3335.text: (1) specify the documents or the classes of documents which the respondent must produce; and

3336.text: (2) require him, when producing the documents , to specify any of those documents :

3337.text: (a) which are no longer in his control; or (b) in respect of which he claims a right or duty to withhold production.

3340.text: (1) require the respondent to indicate what has happened to any documents which are no longer in his control; and

3341.text: (2) specify the time and place for production.

3342.text: Orders For Production Of Documents Against A Person Not A Party

3344.text: An application for production of documents by a person who is not a party to the proceedings must under these Rules be supported by evidence.

3346.text: The Court may make an order under this rule only where:

3347.text: (1) the documents of which production is sought are likely to support the case of the applicant or adversely affect the case of one of the other parties to the proceedings; and

3348.text: (2) production is necessary in order to dispose fairly of the claim or to save costs.

3351.text: (1) specify the documents or the classes of documents which the respondent must produce; and (2) require the respondent, when producing the documents , to specify any of the documents which the Court has ordered should be produced:

3352.text: (a) which are no longer in his control; or (b) in respect of which he claims a right or duty to withhold production.

3355.text: (1) require the respondent to indicate what has happened to any documents which are no longer in his control; and

3356.text: (2) specify the time and place for production.

3357.text: Rules Not To Limit Other Powers Of The Court To Order Production Of Documents

3359.text: Rules 28.47 to 28.54 do not limit any other power which the Court may have to order:

3360.text: (1) production of documents before proceedings have started; and (2) production of documents against a person who is not a party to proceedings.

3361.text: Request To Produce Documents By The Court On Its Own Initiative

3363.text: The Court may at any time request a party to produce to the Court and to the other parties any documents that it considers to be relevant and material to the outcome of the case.

3365.text: A party may object to such a request based on any of the reasons set out in Rule 28.28 .

3367.text: If a party raises such an objection, the Court shall decide whether to order the production of such documents based upon the considerations set out in Rule 28.28 and, if the Court considers it appropriate, through the use of the procedures set out in Rule 28.41.

3370.text: Within the time ordered by the Court , the parties may produce to the other parties any additional documents which they believe have become relevant and material as a consequence of the issues raised in statements of case, documents , witness statements or expert reports submitted or produced by another party or in other submissions of the parties.

3371.text: Consequence Of Failure To Produce Documents

3373.text: A party may not rely on any document which he fails to produce unless the Court gives permission.

3375.text: If a party fails without satisfactory explanation to produce any document requested in a Request to Produce to which he has not objected in due time or fails to produce any document ordered to be produced by the

3376.text: Court , the Court may infer that such document would be adverse to the interests of that party.

3379.text: Proceedings for contempt of court may be brought against a person if he makes, or causes to be made, a false Document Production Statement, without an honest belief in its truth.

3381.text: Proceedings under Rule 28.62 may be brought only with the permission of the Court .

3384.text: A party to whom a document has been produced may use the document only for the purpose of the proceedings in which it has been produced, except where:

3385.text: (1) the document has been read to or by the Court , or referred to, at a hearing which has been held in public;

3387.text: (3) the party who produced the document and the person to whom the document belongs agree.

3389.text: The Court may make an order restricting or prohibiting the use of a document which has been produced, even where the document has been read to or by the Court , or referred to, at a hearing which has been held in public.

3391.text: An application for such an order may be made:

3393.text: (2) by any person to whom the document belongs.

3394.text: Restriction On Use Of A Privileged Document Inspection Of Which Has Been Inadvertently Allowed

3396.text: Where a party inadvertently allows a privileged document to be inspected, the party who has inspected the document may use it or its contents only with the permission of the Court.

3397.text: Schedule A to Part 28 Document Production Schedule

3402.text: This Part sets out how evidence is to be given and facts are to be proved.

3404.text: Evidence at a hearing other than the trial should normally be given by witness statement. A witness statement is a written statement signed by a person which contains the evidence that person would have otherwise submitted orally.

3406.text: A witness may give evidence by affidavit if he wishes to do so.

3408.text: Statements of case and application notices may also be used as evidence provided that their contents have been verified by a statement of truth.

3410.text: Affidavits must be used as evidence in the following instances:

3411.text: (1) where sworn evidence is required by an enactment, Rule, order or Practice Direction;

3412.text: (2) in any application for a search order, a freezing order, or an order requiring an occupier to permit another to enter his land; and

3413.text: (3) in any application for an order against anyone for alleged contempt of court.

3415.text: If a party believes that sworn evidence is required by a court in another jurisdiction for any purpose connected with the proceedings, he may apply to the Court for a direction that evidence shall be given only by affidavit in any pre-trial applications.

3416.text: Schedule B to Part 28 Document Production Statement

3418.text: The Court may give a direction that evidence shall be given by affidavit instead of, or in addition to, a witness statement or statement of case :

3419.text: (1) on its own initiative; or (2) after any party has applied to the Court for such a direction.

3421.text: An affidavit , where referred to in these Rules or a Practice Direction, also means an affirmation unless the context requires otherwise.

3422.text: Power Of Court To Control Evidence

3424.text: The Court may control the evidence by giving directions as to:

3425.text: (1) the issues on which it requires evidence; (2) the nature of the evidence which it requires to decide those issues; and (3) the way in which the evidence is to be placed before the Court .

3427.text: The Court may use its power under Rule 29.9 to exclude evidence that would otherwise be admissible.

3432.text: The general rule is that any fact which needs to be proved by the evidence of witnesses is to be proved:

3433.text: (1) at trial, by their oral evidence given in public; and (2) at any other hearing, by their evidence in writing.

3436.text: (1) to any provision to the contrary contained in these Rules or elsewhere; or

3437.text: (2) to any order of the Court .

3438.text: Evidence By Video Link Or Other Means

3440.text: The Court may allow a witness to give evidence through a video link or by other means.

3441.text: Defects In Affidavits, Witness Statements And Exhibits

3446.text: (3) an exhibit to either an affidavit or a witness statement;

3447.text: does not comply with this Part in relation to its form, the Court may refuse to admit it as evidence and may refuse to allow the costs arising from its preparation.

3449.text: Permission to file a defective affidavit or witness statement or to use a defective exhibit must be obtained from a Judge.

3451.text: Preparation And Form Of Witness Statements

3453.text: A witness statement must comply with the requirements set out in this section of this Part.

3456.text: (1) the function of a witness statement is to set out in writing the evidence in chief of the witness; as far as possible, therefore, the statement should be in the witness’s own words;

3457.text: (2) it should be as concise as the circumstances of the case allow without omitting any significant matters;

3458.text: (3) it should not contain lengthy quotations from documents;

3459.text: (4) it should not engage in argument;

3460.text: (5) it must indicate which of the statements made in it are made from the witness’s own knowledge and which are made on information or belief, giving the source for any statement made on information or belief; and

3461.text: (6) it must contain a statement by the witness that he believes the matters stated in it are true; proceedings for contempt of court may be brought against a person if he makes, or causes to be made, a false statement in a witness statement without an honest belief in its truth.

3463.text: It is improper to put pressure of any kind on a witness to give anything other than his own account of the matters with which his statement deals. It is also improper to serve a witness statement which is known to be false or which it is known the maker does not in all respects actually believe to be true.

3466.text: If a witness is not sufficiently fluent in English to give his evidence in English, the witness statement should be in the witness’s own language and a translation provided.

3468.text: If a witness is not fluent in English but can make himself understood in broken English and can understand written English, the statement need not be in his own words provided that these matters are indicated in the statement itself. It must however be written so as to express as accurately as possible the substance of his evidence.

3471.text: The witness statement should be headed with the title of the proceedings where the proceedings are between several parties with the same status it is sufficient to identify the parties as follows:

3473.text: A.B. (and others) Claimants/Applicants C.D. (and others) Defendants/Respondents (as appropriate)

3475.text: At the top right hand corner of the first page (and on the backsheet) there should be clearly written:

3477.text: (5) the date the statement was made.

3480.text: The witness statement must, if practicable, be in the intended witness’s own words, the statement should be expressed in the first person and should also state:

3484.text: (1) which of the statements in it are made from the witness’s own knowledge and which are matters of information or belief; and

3485.text: (2) the source for any matters of information or belief.

3487.text: An exhibit used in conjunction with a witness statement should be verified and identified by the witness and remain separate from the witness statement.

3489.text: Where a witness refers to an exhibit or exhibits, he should state “I refer to the (description of exhibit) marked ‘…’”.

3491.text: The provisions of Rules 29.85 to 29.98 (exhibits) apply similarly to witness statements as they do to affidavits .

3493.text: Where a witness makes more than one witness statement to which there are exhibits, in the same proceedings, the numbering of the exhibits should run consecutively throughout and not start again with each witness statement.

3497.text: (1) be fully legible and should normally be typed on one side of the paper only;

3498.text: (2) where possible, be bound securely in a manner which would not hamper filing , or otherwise each page should be endorsed with the case number and should bear the initials of the witness;

3499.text: (3) have the pages numbered consecutively as a separate statement (or as one of several statements contained in a file);

3501.text: (5) have all numbers, including dates, expressed in figures; and

3502.text: (6) give the reference to any document or documents mentioned either in the margin or in bold text in the body of the statement.

3504.text: It is usually convenient for a witness statement to follow the chronological sequence of the events or matters dealt with, each paragraph of a witness statement should as far as possible be confined to a distinct portion of the subject.

3507.text: A witness statement is the equivalent of the oral evidence which that witness would, if called, give in evidence; it must include a statement by the intended witness that he believes the facts in it are true.

3509.text: To verify a witness statement the statement of truth is as follows:

3510.text: “I believe that the facts stated in this witness statement are true”.

3512.text: Attention is drawn to Section VI of this Part which sets out the consequences of verifying a witness statement containing a false statement without an honest belief in its truth.

3515.text: Any alteration to a witness statement must be initialled by the person making the statement or by the authorised person where appropriate.

3517.text: A witness statement which contains an alteration that has not been initialled may be used in evidence only with the permission of the Court .

3520.text: If the Court directs that a witness statement is to be filed, it must be filed in the Registry .

3522.text: Where the Court has directed that a witness statement in a language other than English is to be filed:

3523.text: (1) the party wishing to rely on it must:

3524.text: (a) have it translated; and (b) file the witness statement in its original language with the Court ; and

3525.text: (2) the translator must make and file with the Court an affidavit verifying the translation and exhibiting both the translation and a copy of the witness statement in its original language.

3526.text: Requirement To Serve Witness Statements For Use At Trial

3528.text: The Court will order a party to serve on the other parties any witness statement of the oral evidence which the party serving the statement intends to rely on in relation to any issues of fact to be decided at the trial.

3530.text: The Court may give directions as to:

3531.text: (1) the order in which witness statements are to be served; and (2) whether or not the witness statements are to be filed.

3532.text: Use At Trial Of Witness Statements Which Have Been Served

3535.text: (1) a party has served a witness statement; and

3536.text: (2) he wishes to rely at trial on the evidence of the witness who made the statement;

3537.text: he must call the witness to give oral evidence unless the Court orders otherwise or he puts the statement in as hearsay evidence.

3539.text: Where a witness is called to give oral evidence under Rule 29.41, his witness statement shall stand as his evidence in chief unless the Court orders otherwise.

3541.text: In an appropriate case the trial Judge may direct that the whole or any part of a witness’s evidence in chief is to be given orally. Any application for such an order should be made at the beginning of the trial.

3543.text: A witness giving oral evidence at trial may with the permission of the Court:

3545.text: (2) give evidence in relation to new matters which have arisen since the witness statement was served on the other parties.

3547.text: The Court will give permission under Rule 29.44 only if it considers that there is good reason not to confine the evidence of the witness to the contents of his witness statement.

3549.text: A supplemental witness statement should normally be served where the witness proposes materially to add to, alter, correct or retract from what is in his original statement. Permission will be required for the service of a supplemental statement.

3551.text: A party who has decided not to call to give oral evidence at trial a witness whose statement has been served must give prompt notice of this decision to all other parties. He must at the same time state whether he proposes to put the statement in as hearsay evidence.

3553.text: If a party who has served a witness statement does not:

3554.text: (1) call the witness to give evidence at trial; or (2) put the witness statement in as hearsay evidence; any other party may put the witness statement in as hearsay evidence.

3557.text: Where a witness is called to give evidence at trial, he may be cross-examined on his witness statement,

3558.text: whether or not the statement or any part of it was referred to during the witness’s evidence in chief .

3562.text: (1) is required to serve a witness statement for use at trial; but

3563.text: (2) is unable to obtain one, may apply, without notice, for permission to serve a witness summary instead.

3565.text: A witness summary is a summary of:

3566.text: (1) the evidence, if known, which would otherwise be included in a witness statement; or (2) if the evidence is not known, the matters about which the party serving the witness summary proposes to question the witness.

3568.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise, a witness summary must include the name and address of the intended witness.

3570.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise, a witness summary must be served within the period in which a witness statement would have had to be served.

3572.text: Where a party serves a witness summary, so far as practicable the provisions of this section of this Part with regard to the form of witness statement and Rules 29.39 and 29.40 (requirement to serve witness statements for use at trial) and 29.44 (amplifying witness statements) shall apply to the summary.

3573.text: Consequence Of Failure To Serve Witness Statement Or Summary

3575.text: If a witness statement or a witness summary for use at trial is not served in respect of an intended witness within the time specified by the Court , then the witness may not be called to give oral evidence unless the Court gives permission.

3576.text: Evidence In Proceedings Other Than At Trial

3578.text: Subject to Rule 29.57, the general rule is that evidence at hearings other than the trial is to be by witness

3579.text: statement unless the Court , a Practice Direction or any other enactment requires otherwise.

3581.text: At hearings other than the trial, a party may, rely on the matters set out in:

3582.text: (1) his statement of case ; or (2) his application notice ;

3583.text: if the statement of case or application notice is verified by a statement of truth.

3586.text: Where, at a hearing other than the trial, evidence is given in writing, any party may apply to the Court for permission to cross-examine the person giving the evidence.

3588.text: If the Court gives permission under Rule 29.58 but the person in question does not attend as required by the order, his evidence may not be used unless the Court gives permission.

3589.text: Use Of Witness Statements For Other Purposes

3591.text: A witness statement may be used only for the purpose of the proceedings in which it is served unless:

3592.text: (1) the witness gives consent in writing to some other use of it; (2) the Court gives permission for some other use; or (3) the witness statement has been put in evidence at a hearing held in public.

3593.text: Availability Of Witness Statements For Inspection

3595.text: A witness statement which stands as evidence in chief is open to inspection during the course of the trial unless the Court otherwise directs.

3597.text: Any person may ask for a direction that a witness statement is not open to inspection.

3599.text: The Court will not make a direction under Rule 29.62 unless it is satisfied that a witness statement should not be open to inspection because of:

3602.text: The Court may exclude from inspection words or passages in the statement.

3605.text: Where the Court has ordered that a witness statement is not to be open to inspection by the public or that words or passages in the statement are not to be open to inspection, a Court Officer will so certify on the statement and make any deletions directed by the Court under Rule 29.64.

3608.text: Evidence must be given by affidavit instead of or in addition to a witness statement if this is required by the Court , a provision contained in any other Rule, a Practice Direction or any other enactment.

3610.text: Nothing in these Rules prevents a witness giving evidence by affidavit at a hearing other than the trial if he chooses to do so in a case where Rule 29.66 does not apply, but the party putting forward the affidavit may not recover the additional cost of making it from any other party unless the Court orders otherwise.

3613.text: A deponent is a person who gives evidence by affidavit or affirmation.

3616.text: The affidavit should be headed with the title of the proceedings; where the proceedings are between several parties with the same status it is sufficient to identify the parties as follows:

3618.text: A.B. (and others) Claimants/Applicants C.D. (and others) Defendants/Respondents

3621.text: At the top right hand corner of the first page (and on the backsheet) there should be clearly written:

3625.text: The affidavit must, if practicable, be in the deponent’s own words, the affidavit should be expressed in the first person and the deponent should:

3629.text: (1) which of the statements in it are made from the deponent’s own knowledge and which are matters of information or belief; and

3630.text: (2) the source for any matters of information or belief.

3633.text: (1) refers to an exhibit or exhibits, he should state ‘there is now shown to me marked ‘…’ the (description of exhibit)’, and

3634.text: (2) makes more than one affidavit (to which there are exhibits) in the same proceedings, the numbering of the exhibits should run consecutively throughout and not start again with each affidavit .

3637.text: The jurat of an affidavit is a statement set out at the end of the document which authenticates the affidavit . The general form of jurat is set out in the Schedule to this Part.

3641.text: (2) be completed and signed by the person before whom the affidavit was sworn whose name and qualification must be printed beneath his signature;

3642.text: (3) contain the full address of the person before whom the affidavit was sworn; and

3643.text: (4) follow immediately on from the text and not be put on a separate page.

3648.text: (2) be fully legible and should normally be typed on one side of the paper only;

3649.text: (3) where possible, be bound securely in a manner which would not hamper filing , or otherwise each page should be endorsed with the case number and should bear the initials of the deponent and of the person before whom it was sworn;

3650.text: (4) have the pages numbered consecutively as a separate document (or as one of several documents contained in a file);

3652.text: (6) have all numbers, including dates, expressed in figures; and

3653.text: (7) give the reference to any document or documents mentioned either in the margin or in bold text in the body of the affidavit .

3655.text:
It is usually convenient for an affidavit to follow the chronological sequence of events or matters dealt with;   
each paragraph of an affidavit should as far as possible be confined to a distinct portion of the subject.

3656.text: Inability Of Deponent To Read Or Sign Affidavit

3658.text: Where an affidavit is sworn by a person who is unable to read or sign it, the person before whom the affidavit is sworn must certify in the jurat that:

3659.text: (1) he read the affidavit to the deponent; (2) the deponent appeared to understand it; and (3) the deponent signed or made his mark in his presence.

3661.text: If that certificate is not included in the jurat , the affidavit may not be used in evidence unless the Court is satisfied that it was read to the deponent and that he appeared to understand it. Two versions of the form of jurat with the certificate are set out in the Schedule to this Part.

3664.text: Any alteration to an affidavit must be initialled by both the deponent and the person before whom the affidavit was sworn.

3666.text: An affidavit which contains an alteration that has not been initialled may be filed or used in evidence only with the permission of the Court .

3667.text: Who May Administer Oaths And Take Affidavits

3669.text: Only the following may administer oaths and take affidavits :

3672.text: 29.83 If the Court directs that an affidavit is to be filed, it must be filed in the Registry .

3674.text: Where an affidavit is in a language other than English:

3675.text: (1) the party wishing to rely on it:

3677.text: (b) must file the affidavit in its original language with the Court ; and

3678.text: (2) the translator must make and file with the Court an affidavit verifying the translation and exhibiting both the translation and a copy of the affidavit in its original language.

3682.text: A document used in conjunction with an affidavit should be:

3683.text: (1) produced to and verified by the deponent, and remain separate from the affidavit ; and (2) identified by a declaration of the person before whom the affidavit was sworn.

3685.text: The declaration should be headed with the name of the proceedings in the same way as the affidavit .

3687.text: The first page of each exhibit should be marked:

3689.text: (2) with the exhibit mark referred to in the affidavit .

3692.text: Copies of individual letters should be collected together and exhibited in a bundle or bundles. They should be arranged in chronological order with the earliest at the top, and firmly secured.

3694.text: When a bundle of correspondence is exhibited, the exhibit should have a front page attached stating that the bundle consists of original letters and copies. They should be arranged and secured as above and numbered consecutively.

3697.text: Photocopies instead of original documents may be exhibited provided the originals are made available for inspection by the other parties before the hearing and by the Judge at the hearing.

3699.text: Court documents must not be exhibited (official copies of such documents prove themselves).

3701.text: Where an exhibit contains more than one document, a front page should be attached setting out a list of the documents contained in the exhibit; the list should contain the dates of the documents.

3704.text: Items other than documents should be clearly marked with an exhibit number or letter in such a manner that the mark cannot become detached from the exhibit.

3706.text: Small items may be placed in a container and the container appropriately marked.

3709.text: Where an exhibit contains more than one document:

3710.text: (1) the bundle should not be stapled but should be securely fastened in a way that does not hinder the reading of the documents; and

3711.text: (2) the pages should be numbered consecutively at bottom centre.

3713.text: Every page of an exhibit should be clearly legible; typed copies of illegible documents should be included, paginated with ‘a’ numbers.

3715.text: Where affidavits and exhibits have become numerous, they should be put into separate bundles and the pages numbered consecutively throughout.

3717.text: Where on account of their bulk the service of exhibits or copies of exhibits on the other parties would be difficult or impracticable, the directions of the Court should be sought as to arrangements for bringing the exhibits to the attention of the other parties and as to their custody pending trial.

3720.text: All provisions in these Rules relating to affidavits apply to affirmations with the following exceptions:

3721.text: (1) the deponent should commence ‘I (name) of (address) do solemnly and sincerely affirm ……’, and (2) in the jurat the word ‘sworn’ is replaced by the word ‘affirmed’.

3724.text: A person may make an affidavit outside the DIFC in accordance with:

3725.text: (1) this Part; or (2) the law of the place where he makes the affidavit .

3730.text: (1) ‘hearsay’ means a statement made, otherwise than by a person while giving oral evidence in proceedings, which is tendered as evidence of the matters stated; and

3731.text: (2) references to hearsay include hearsay of whatever degree.

3732.text: Notice Of Intention To Rely On Hearsay Evidence

3734.text: Where a party intends to rely on hearsay evidence at trial and either:

3735.text:
(1) that evidence is to be given by a witness giving oral evidence; or   
(2) that evidence is contained in a witness statement of a person who is not being called to give oral evidence;

3736.text: that party will be deemed to give notice of such an intention by serving a witness statement on the other parties in accordance with the Court’s order.

3738.text: Where Rule 29.102(2) applies, the party intending to rely on the hearsay evidence must, when he serves the witness statement:

3739.text: (1) inform the other parties that the witness is not being called to give oral evidence; and

3740.text: (2) give the reason why the witness will not be called.
